abstracts.title: Insurance Requirements
abstracts.desc: Association master policy and unit owner coverage obligations under the Declaration and Bylaws.
abstracts.subtitle: The Association maintains comprehensive insurance for common areas and facilities, while unit owners are responsible for personal property and unit-specific risks.

blocks.heading: Association Insurance Policies
blocks.text: The Association is required to maintain comprehensive insurance for the common areas and facilities of the condominium. These policies are funded as a common expense through assessments based on each owner’s percentage of ownership.

cards.label: Fire Insurance
cards.text: Full insurable replacement value of common areas and facilities, without deduction for depreciation.

cards.label: Liability Insurance
cards.text: $200,000 per person injured, $500,000 per accident, and $50,000 for property damage. Limits reviewed annually.

cards.label: Workers' Comp
cards.text: As required by Washington state laws for employees or contractors on Association-managed property.

cards.label: Fidelity Bonds
cards.text: For Board members and relevant personnel to protect against financial misconduct.

sourceText.label: View exact policy language
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Article 12.1
passages: The Association must secure fire insurance with extended coverage endorsement, covering the full insurable replacement value of the common areas and facilities, without deduction for depreciation.
Liability insurance with minimum coverage limits of $200,000 per person injured, $500,000 per accident, and $50,000 for property damage. These limits are subject to annual review.
Workers’ compensation insurance as required by applicable Washington state laws.
Fidelity bonds for Board members and other relevant personnel to protect against financial misconduct.

blocks.heading: Your Insurance Obligations
blocks.text: The Association’s policies do not cover your personal belongings or unit-specific risks. You need your own coverage for everything inside your unit.

cards.label: Personal Property
cards.text: Insurance for all personal property within your unit — the Association’s policy does not cover individual belongings.

cards.label: Improvements Over $1,000
cards.text: Notify the Board of any improvements valued over $1,000 and file copies of insurance policies covering them.

cards.label: Unit-Specific Liability
cards.text: Coverage for damages caused by plumbing fixtures, appliances, or equipment within your unit, including hot water tanks.

sourceText.label: View exact owner requirements
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Article 12 (Pages 73–74)
passages: Owners must secure insurance for personal property within their units, as the Association’s policies do not cover individual belongings.
Owners are required to notify the Board of any improvements to their unit valued over $1,000, as these may affect the overall insurable value of the condominium.
Owners must file copies of any individual insurance policies covering such improvements with the Association.
Owners are responsible for insuring against risks specific to their units, such as damages caused by plumbing fixtures, appliances, or other equipment within their unit.

action.label: If damage occurs in your unit
action.title: What to do when you need to file a claim
steps: Document the damage — take photos, note the date, and identify the source.
Determine responsibility — if it’s within your unit (plumbing, appliances, personal property), your insurance applies. If it involves common areas, contact management.
File with your insurer — you are responsible for unit-specific losses.
Notify Phillips Real Estate — (206) 622-8600 — if the damage affects common areas or other units.

blocks.heading: Special Conditions
items: The Association’s insurer’s liability is not affected by other insurance obtained by unit owners — no set-off, counterclaim, or contribution by reason of additional owner policies.
Policies include a mortgagee clause protecting institutional first mortgagees. Settlements exceeding $2,500 require mortgagee approval.
Insurance policies must not be affected by hazardous use or vacancy of the property — continuous coverage regardless of occupancy.
In cases of damage, the Board may authorize emergency work funded by insurance proceeds or assessments.

sourceText.label: View exact provisions
sourceText.heading: Declaration — Articles 12–13
passages: The Association’s insurance policies must include provisions ensuring that the insurer’s liability is not affected by other insurance obtained by unit owners. This prevents set-off, counterclaim, apportionment, proration, or contribution by reason of additional owner policies.

Policies must include a mortgagee clause that protects institutional first mortgagees. Any settlement exceeding $2,500 requires mortgagee approval, provided such approval is not unreasonably withheld.
Insurance policies must not be affected by hazardous use or vacancy of the property, ensuring continuous coverage regardless of occupancy status.
In cases of damage or destruction, the Board may authorize emergency work to prevent further damage, funded by insurance proceeds or assessments (Article 13.3.2).

blocks.heading: Board Oversight & Owner Compliance
items: The Board annually reviews and adjusts insurance coverage limits to ensure adequacy.
Owners must promptly notify the Board of improvements exceeding $1,000 and provide copies of individual insurance policies.
Insurance costs are included in common expense assessments based on ownership percentage.
Non-payment of assessments can lead to liens or foreclosure.
Owners must ensure tenants comply with insurance-related obligations.

takeaways: The Association maintains fire, liability, workers’ comp, and fidelity bond insurance for common areas — funded through your assessments.
You must insure your personal property and unit-specific risks. Notify the Board of any improvements over $1,000.
Mortgagee protections are built into all policies — settlements over $2,500 need mortgagee approval.
The Board reviews coverage annually. Non-payment of assessments can lead to liens or foreclosure.
Contact Phillips Real Estate (206-622-8600) for insurance questions or to report damage.

abstracts.title: Maintenance Responsibilities
abstracts.desc: Unit owner and Association upkeep obligations, emergency procedures, and enforcement.
abstracts.subtitle: Rules for maintaining units and common areas, balancing individual owner obligations with collective responsibilities to minimize costs and preserve property value.

blocks.heading: What You Maintain
blocks.text: You are responsible for everything inside your unit and assigned limited common areas like decks. You must also ensure your tenants comply with all rules.
items: Interior maintenance — appliances, plumbing, electrical fixtures, wiring, water heaters, wall and floor coverings, and mailboxes.
Limited common areas — decks and lanais must be maintained without modification or damage (no drilling, no heavy planters directly on surfaces).
Tenant oversight — provide tenants with House Rules and you are liable for any tenant-caused damage.
Leak prevention — turn off washer valves when away, maintain hot water tanks, and avoid actions increasing insurance rates.
Move-in/move-out — coordinate with management, pay $250 fee, and remove trash promptly.

sourceText.label: View exact responsibilities from governing documents
sourceText.heading: Declaration & House Rules
passages: Owners are responsible for maintaining, repairing, and replacing all elements within their unit, including appliances, plumbing, electrical fixtures, wiring, water heaters, wall and floor coverings, and mailboxes.

Owners must maintain assigned limited common areas (e.g., decks, lanais) without modifying or damaging them. No drilling into exterior stucco. Heavy planters must be supported on foam or neoprene and precast concrete pavers.
Owners leasing units must ensure tenants comply with all rules, provide tenants with House Rules, and are liable for tenant-caused damage.
Owners must prevent leaks (e.g., turn off washer valves when away, maintain hot water tanks), avoid actions increasing insurance rates, and obtain Board approval for structural or exterior changes.

action.label: If something needs repair
action.title: How to handle a maintenance issue
steps: Determine responsibility — interior components and unit-exclusive systems are yours. Common areas and building systems are the Association’s.
If it’s yours — arrange repairs directly. Get Board approval for any exterior or structural work.
If it’s the Association’s — contact Phillips Real Estate at (206) 622-8600.
If it’s urgent — report immediately. You may be liable for damage caused by delay.

blocks.heading: What the Association Maintains
blocks.text: The HOA handles all common areas, building-wide systems, and exterior components.

cards.label: Common Areas
cards.text: Hallways, stairwells, lobbies, garages, grounds, and roofs.

cards.label: Exterior
cards.text: Building exterior components including railings and exterior deck surfaces.

cards.label: Building Systems
cards.text: Shared water, sewer, and electrical services affecting the entire building.

cards.label: Administration
cards.text: Reserve accounts, budgets, assessments, and management of maintenance operations.

blocks.heading: Emergency Repairs
blocks.text: The HOA can authorize immediate repairs to prevent further damage or safety risks. For significant emergencies, the Board may confer via phone or email to expedite decisions.

pullQuote.text: Report urgent issues — leaks, structural damage — to Phillips Real Estate at (206) 622-8600 for prompt action.
pullQuote.caption: Emergency contact

sourceText.label: View emergency provisions
sourceText.heading: Declaration & Bylaws
passages: The HOA can authorize immediate repairs for emergencies to prevent further damage or ensure safety, with costs potentially covered by reserve funds or special assessments.
For significant emergencies, the Board may confer via phone or email to expedite decisions.
The Board may enter apartments for emergency repairs or maintenance with minimal inconvenience, with immediate entry permitted in emergencies (Bylaws, Article 6.3).

blocks.heading: How Maintenance Is Funded
blocks.text: Your monthly assessments cover routine maintenance and building operations. A reserve account handles unexpected costs.
items: Monthly assessments based on ownership percentage cover water, sewer, garbage, and common area upkeep.
A reserve account is maintained for unforeseen or unbudgeted costs.
Special assessments may be levied for major repairs — expenses over $5,000 may require owner approval.
Unpaid assessments or fines result in liens on the unit.

cards.label: Reserved Parking
cards.text: $44–$110 depending on space.

cards.label: Second Infraction
cards.text: Minimum $50 fine.

cards.label: Ongoing Violations
cards.text: Minimum $200 fine per occurrence until corrected.

cards.label: Tenant Violations
cards.text: $100 per occurrence.

cards.label: Unapproved Rental
cards.text: $200 fine.

blocks.heading: What Happens If You Don’t Comply
blocks.text: Non-compliance carries real financial consequences — fines, repair costs, liens, and potential legal action.
items: First infraction receives a warning. Second infraction incurs a minimum $50 fine. Ongoing violations incur $200+ fines.
Owners are financially responsible for damage to common or limited common areas caused by themselves, tenants, or guests.
Deck damage from negligence could exceed $8,000 in repair costs.
Non-payment of fines within 30 days leads to a lien on the unit.
The HOA may pursue legal action, with owners liable for attorney fees and costs.
Non-compliance with rental rules may result in loss of rental privileges or termination of parking agreements.

pullQuote.text: Deck damage from negligence could exceed $8,000 in repair costs. Owners are fully liable for damage caused by themselves, tenants, or guests.
pullQuote.caption: Potential repair liability

takeaways: You maintain your unit interior and assigned limited common areas (decks). The HOA handles common areas and building systems.
Deck maintenance is critical — no drilling, no heavy planters on bare surfaces, no glued carpet. Damage from negligence can cost $8,000+.
Emergency repairs go through Phillips Real Estate at (206) 622-8600.
Fines escalate: warning, then $50, then $200+ per occurrence. Non-payment within 30 days becomes a lien.
You are responsible for your tenants’ compliance. Tenant violations cost $100 each.
Get Board approval for all exterior or structural changes before starting work.

abstracts.title: ACC Requests
abstracts.desc: Architectural Control Committee approval process for exterior, structural, and common area modifications.
abstracts.subtitle: The Board of Directors manages architectural control to ensure uniform exterior appearance, structural integrity, and compliance with governing documents.

blocks.heading: What Requires Approval?
blocks.text: All modifications to your unit’s exterior, structure, or anything impacting common or limited common elements need prior written Board approval.
items: Exterior modifications — painting, alterations to decks, doors, windows, or any exterior-visible portion of a unit.
Structural changes — alterations to structural components, including subdividing or combining units.
Common and limited common areas — attaching plant boxes, installing carpet, or adding structures like trellises to hallways, stairwells, roof decks, or unit decks.
Signs — displaying signs, notices, or advertisements, except for approved “For Sale” signs.

sourceText.label: View exact requirements
sourceText.heading: House Rules & Declaration
passages: Exterior Modifications: Any painting, modification, or alteration to the building, decks, doors, windows, or other exterior-visible portions of a unit (House Rules, Rule 17; Declaration, Section 10.5).
Structural Changes: Alterations to structural components of a unit, including subdividing or combining units (Declaration, Section 24.1).

Common and Limited Common Areas: Changes to common areas or limited common areas, such as attaching plant boxes, installing carpet, or adding structures like trellises (House Rules, Rule 16; Declaration, Section 10.11).
Signs: Displaying signs, notices, or advertisements in units or common areas, except for approved “For Sale” signs (House Rules, Rule 5, Addendum 2).
sourceText.ref: Shannon Heights House Rules, Rules 5, 16, 17; Declaration, Sections 10.5, 10.11, 24.1

pullQuote.text: Structural changes like subdividing or combining units require a vote of owners holding more than 66⅔% of the voting power.
pullQuote.caption: Major modification threshold — Declaration, Section 24.1.2

action.label: Before you start any modification
action.title: ACC approval step by step
steps: Contact Phillips Real Estate — (206) 622-8600 — to submit your written request with detailed plans and specifications.
Board reviews for compliance — the Board evaluates your request against the Declaration, Bylaws, and House Rules.
Obtain additional approvals if needed — you may need Declaration amendments, Lake Forest Park city building permits, insurance coverage, and other provisions.
Receive written approval — do not begin work until you have written Board consent.
For structural changes — approval requires 66⅔% of voting power.

blocks.heading: Exterior & Deck Rules
blocks.text: Deck maintenance and exterior uniformity are strictly enforced at Shannon Heights. These rules exist because deck damage is expensive to repair.
items: Modifications must not deviate from the original building appearance — deck coatings and colors are controlled by the Board.
No screws, nails, or adhesives may penetrate deck surfaces.
Heavy planters must be supported on foam or neoprene and precast concrete pavers.
Outdoor carpeting requires Board approval — must be loose-laid, non-absorbent, and removed seasonally.
No drilling into exterior stucco.
No storing unsightly items or attaching lattice/trellises to deck railings.
Hot grease or caustic/abrasive materials are banned on decks.
All changes must comply with King County and Lake Forest Park regulations.

sourceText.label: View full deck rules
sourceText.heading: House Rules — Rule 16
passages: No screws, nails, or adhesives may penetrate deck surfaces. Heavy planters must be supported on foam or neoprene and precast concrete pavers.
Outdoor carpeting requires Board approval and must be loose-laid, non-absorbent, and removed seasonally.
No drilling into exterior stucco, storing unsightly items, or attaching lattice/trellises to deck railings.
Hot grease or caustic/abrasive materials are banned on decks.
Deck coatings and colors are controlled by the Board to maintain uniform exterior appearance.

blocks.heading: Non-Compliance
blocks.text: Violations follow a structured enforcement process with escalating penalties.
items: First infraction: warning.
Second infraction: minimum $50 fine.
Continuing infractions: minimum $200 fine until corrected.
Owners are liable for repair costs due to negligence or unauthorized changes — deck damage can exceed $8,000.
Unpaid fines or repair costs result in a lien against the unit.
Owners are responsible for attorney fees and costs if legal action is needed.

sourceText.label: View exact penalty language
sourceText.heading: House Rules — Addendum 4
passages: Violations incur a warning for the first infraction, a minimum $50 fine for the second, and a minimum $200 fine for continuing infractions until corrected.
Owners are liable for repair costs due to negligence or unauthorized changes, e.g., deck damage from improper planter placement or glued carpet.
Unpaid fines or repair costs result in a lien against the owner’s unit.
Owners are responsible for attorney fees and costs if legal action is needed to enforce compliance.
sourceText.ref: Shannon Heights House Rules, Addendum 4; Declaration, Sections 11.2, 18.1

takeaways: All exterior, structural, and common area modifications require prior written Board approval.
Deck rules are strict — no penetrating surfaces, no glued carpet, no drilling into stucco. Damage can cost $8,000+.
Structural changes (subdividing/combining units) require 66⅔% owner voting approval.
Fines escalate from warning to $50 to $200+ per occurrence. Unpaid amounts become liens.
Contact Phillips Real Estate (206-622-8600) to submit ACC requests.

abstracts.title: Board of Directors
abstracts.desc: Governance structure, elections, authority, and current board composition.
abstracts.subtitle: The Board of Directors manages operations, enforces rules, oversees finances, maintains common areas, and handles legal matters for Shannon Heights.

cards.label: Board Size
cards.text: Five (5) members after the Declarant’s management authority ends.

cards.label: Officers
cards.text: President, Vice-President, Secretary, and Treasurer — elected annually by the Board.

cards.label: Terms
cards.text: 2-year terms. Vacancies filled by majority vote of remaining Board members until next annual meeting.

blocks.heading: How Elections Work
blocks.text: Directors are elected at annual meetings. The initial Board serves a one-year term; subsequent terms are governed by the Bylaws.
items: Vacancies (other than removals) are filled by majority vote of remaining Board members, serving until the next annual meeting.
The Declarant controls the Board until its management authority ends, as specified in the Declaration.

pullQuote.text: Any Board member may be removed, with or without cause, by a majority vote of all apartment owners at a regular or special meeting. The member proposed for removal is given an opportunity to be heard.
pullQuote.caption: Director removal — Bylaws, Article 3.2.3

sourceText.label: View exact removal language
sourceText.heading: Bylaws — Article 3.2.3
passages: Any Board member may be removed, with or without cause, by a majority vote of all apartment owners at a regular or special meeting called for that purpose.
The Board member proposed for removal is given an opportunity to be heard at the meeting.
Removal requires an affirmative vote of a majority of all unit owners, not just those present.

blocks.heading: Meetings & Decision-Making
items: Regular meetings held at least twice per fiscal year, including one immediately following the annual owners’ meeting.
Three days’ notice required for regular and special meetings.
Special meetings called by the President or on written request of at least two Board members.
Majority of Board members constitutes a quorum; majority vote of those present is binding.
Notice may be waived in writing or by attendance.

blocks.heading: Officers of the Board
blocks.text: Officers are elected annually at the organizational meeting following the annual owners’ meeting.

cards.label: President
cards.text: Chief executive — presides over meetings, appoints committees.

cards.label: Vice-President
cards.text: Assumes President’s duties in their absence.

cards.label: Secretary
cards.text: Keeps minutes, maintains records and member lists.

cards.label: Treasurer
cards.text: Oversees financial records, assessments, and deposits.

blocks.heading: Compensation & Liability
blocks.text: Directors serve without compensation. The Association indemnifies Board members and officers for actions taken in good faith, except for willful misconduct. Indemnification does not extend to liabilities incurred as apartment owners.

blocks.heading: What the Board Can Do
blocks.text: The Board has broad authority to manage the Association, including a formal Hearing Board for rule violations.
items: Enforce the Declaration, Bylaws, and House Rules.
Manage common areas — maintenance, repairs, and structural alterations, with authority to access apartments.
Administer assessments, reserve accounts, and unforeseen expenses.
Oversee insurance coverage including fire, liability, and fidelity bonds.
Hire legal, accounting, and management services.
Adopt, amend, or revoke administrative rules and regulations.
Establish a Hearing Board to investigate, hear, and determine complaints regarding violations, with authority to impose fines.

sourceText.label: View Hearing Board process
sourceText.heading: Bylaws — Article 14.4
passages: A Hearing Board is established to investigate, hear, and determine complaints regarding violations of Governing Documents, with authority to impose fines.
The Hearing Board handles complaints, conducts hearings, and issues decisions on violations, with orders effective 10 days after service unless otherwise specified.
The Board may collect rent from tenants to cover delinquent assessments (Declaration Article 11.14).

pullQuote.text: Amendments to the Bylaws require approval by owners holding 66⅔% of the voting power at a regular or special meeting, with at least 10 days’ notice.
pullQuote.caption: Amendment threshold — Bylaws, Article 7.1

takeaways: Five Board members serve 2-year terms. Officers are elected annually.
Any director can be removed by majority vote of all apartment owners — not just those present.
The Board manages finances, enforces rules, and oversees maintenance with authority to access units.
A formal Hearing Board handles violations with the power to impose fines.
Bylaw amendments require 66⅔% owner approval with at least 10 days’ notice.
Directors serve without compensation and are indemnified for good-faith actions.

abstracts.title: Resale Certificate
abstracts.desc: Legally mandated disclosures required for the sale or transfer of any unit.
abstracts.subtitle: A Resale Certificate is required for every unit sale at Shannon Heights, providing buyers with critical financial, governance, and operational disclosures.

blocks.heading: Why It Exists
blocks.text: The Resale Certificate ensures transparency for prospective buyers by disclosing the financial status, governance structure, and obligations of the Shannon Heights Owners Association. It is mandated by the Washington Condominium Act (RCW 64.34.425).

blocks.heading: Who Prepares It?
blocks.text: Phillips Real Estate Services prepares the Resale Certificate at the seller’s request and expense. It must be delivered to the buyer within 10 days of the request.

action.label: If you’re selling your unit
action.title: How to get your Resale Certificate
steps: Contact Phillips Real Estate — (206) 622-8600 — to request the certificate.
Pay the preparation fee — approximately $200–$300 (as of 2017, subject to change).
Certificate delivered within 10 days — this is a legal requirement.
Clear all outstanding amounts — ensure all assessments, fines, or fees are paid before closing.
Buyer receives the certificate before closing — they have 7 days to cancel after receiving it.

blocks.heading: Financial Information
items: Current monthly assessment fees and special assessments.
Outstanding fees or fines on the unit.
Reserve account status.

blocks.heading: Governance & Legal
items: Copies of the Declaration, Bylaws, House Rules, and amendments.
Details of Association insurance coverage, limits, and deductibles.
Pending or threatened litigation involving the Association.
Known violations of House Rules specific to the unit.

blocks.heading: Property & Operations
items: Status of major repairs or capital improvements planned or in progress.
Budget details and reserve study.
Restrictions on unit use or leasing.

pullQuote.text: Buyers have 7 days to cancel the purchase agreement after receiving the Resale Certificate. Cancellation must be in writing. Any earnest money is refunded without penalty.
pullQuote.caption: Buyer cancellation right — RCW 64.34.425(3)

blocks.heading: Fees & Seller Responsibility
blocks.text: The seller pays the preparation fee ($200–$300), must clear all outstanding assessments and fines before transfer, and may owe a $250 move-in/move-out fee. Unpaid amounts may result in a lien.

blocks.heading: Why Compliance Matters
items: Failure to provide a Resale Certificate can delay or void the sale — it’s a legal requirement.
Incomplete or inaccurate certificates may lead to buyer cancellation or legal disputes.
The Association may pursue legal action to enforce payment of outstanding fees, with costs charged to the seller.

takeaways: A Resale Certificate is mandatory for every unit sale — required by Washington law (RCW 64.34.425).
Phillips Real Estate prepares it within 10 days at the seller’s expense ($200–$300).
Buyers have 7 days to cancel after receiving the certificate — with full earnest money refund.
Clear all assessments, fines, and fees before closing to avoid liens or delays.
Contact Phillips Real Estate at (206) 622-8600 to start the process.

abstracts.title: Quick Reference
abstracts.desc: Key numbers, deadlines, and thresholds from all governing documents in one place.
abstracts.subtitle: Every important number, deadline, and threshold from across all Shannon Heights governing document abstracts.

cards.label: Resale Response
cards.text: Association must provide Resale Certificate within 10 days.

cards.label: Buyer Cancellation
cards.text: 7 days to cancel after receiving Resale Certificate.

cards.label: Violation Grace
cards.text: Minimum 10-day period to correct after written notice.

cards.label: Meeting Notice
cards.text: 3 days’ notice for regular and special Board meetings.

cards.label: Amendment Notice
cards.text: At least 10 days’ notice for Bylaw amendment votes.

cards.label: Hearing Board Orders
cards.text: Effective 10 days after service unless otherwise specified.

cards.label: First Violation
cards.text: Warning.

cards.label: Second Violation
cards.text: Minimum $50 fine.

cards.label: Ongoing Violations
cards.text: $200+ per occurrence until corrected.

cards.label: Tenant Violations
cards.text: $100 per occurrence.

cards.label: Unapproved Rental
cards.text: $200 fine.

cards.label: Resale Certificate
cards.text: $200–$300 preparation fee.

cards.label: Deck Damage
cards.text: Can exceed $8,000 in repair costs.

cards.label: Remove Director
cards.text: Majority vote of all apartment owners (not just those present).

cards.label: Amend Bylaws
cards.text: 66⅔% of voting power.

cards.label: Structural Changes
cards.text: 66⅔% of voting power for subdividing/combining units.

cards.label: Board Quorum
cards.text: Majority of Board members.

cards.label: Insurance Settlement
cards.text: Settlements over $2,500 require mortgagee approval.

cards.label: Liability Limits
cards.text: $200K per person / $500K per accident / $50K property damage.

cards.label: Owner Improvements
cards.text: Notify Board of improvements over $1,000.

cards.label: Mortgagee Approval
cards.text: Required for settlements exceeding $2,500.

cards.label: Fire Insurance
cards.text: Full replacement value, no depreciation deduction.

takeaways: Fines escalate: warning → $50 → $200+ per occurrence. Non-payment within 30 days becomes a lien.
Bylaw amendments and major structural changes both require 66⅔% owner approval.
Buyers have 7 days to cancel after receiving the Resale Certificate (vs. 5 days at most other properties).
Liability insurance: $200K/$500K/$50K minimums. Notify the Board of improvements over $1,000.
Board members can be removed by majority of all owners — not just those present at the meeting.
Contact Phillips Real Estate at (206) 622-8600 for all requests and emergencies.
